comments:
Summary:
This paper studies triangulation for mixed stochastic/deterministic graphical models and argues that minimizing treewidth (or restricting to elimination-based triangulations) can be suboptimal when deterministic variables are present. The authors introduce the extra-elimination framework and the notion of ancestral edges, prove that optimal state-space triangulations can be achieved by adding only ancestral edges prior to elimination, provide an algorithm (isEliminationGraph) to test whether a triangulation can be produced by some elimination ordering, and show NP-completeness of the modified state-space triangulation decision problem. They propose several practical heuristics (all-extra, sampled-extra, lo-extra, some-extra), and show empirical results on randomly generated Bayesian networks and several real-world DBNs comparing these heuristics against elimination-based baselines.

Strengths:
- The paper addresses a well-motivated and practically important issue: exploiting determinism during exact inference.
- Theoretical contributions are clear and relevant: demonstration that elimination can miss arbitrarily better triangulations in presence of determinism, the sufficiency of ancestral edges (Theorem 10), and the isEliminationGraph algorithm with a correctness proof. The NP-completeness result for the generalized state-space problem is helpful to justify heuristic approaches.
- The extra-elimination framework and ancestral-edge heuristics are a useful new perspective and appear practical.
- Experimental evaluation covers a substantial set of randomized graphs and several real DBNs; results show substantial speedups in many cases and illustrate when and why the methods help.

Weaknesses / Points for improvement:
- The experimental section, while extensive, lacks some clarity and depth in places: more information on runtime costs of the preprocessing (adding ancestral edges), and on the variability of sampled-extra (how many samples needed) would be useful. It is unclear how sensitive results are to the sampling strategy and heuristic parameters.
- Comparison is largely against elimination-based heuristics. There is limited discussion or comparison to other approaches that exploit determinism or constraint propagation (e.g., zero compression, constraint-based methods), or to non-elimination triangulation heuristics (if any exist). Such comparisons would help position the work relative to alternative inference optimizations.
- The paper acknowledges that choosing which ancestral edges to add is non-local and difficult; more guidance (e.g., simple decision rules, complexity trade-offs, or an ablation study) would strengthen practical applicability.
- Some proofs and lemmas are sketched or referenced; while reasonable for a conference paper, a few more proof sketches or intuition around critical lemmas (for readers unfamiliar with the literature) would help.

Recommendation and scope:
Overall this is a solid contribution to the literature on exact inference in graphical models. The combination of theoretical insight (elimination limitations, sufficiency of ancestral edges) and practical heuristics with empirical validation makes this work of interest to researchers and practitioners working on inference engines, DBNs, and probabilistic graphical models. I recommend this for acceptance (score 8/10). Suggested future work includes deeper empirical analysis of heuristics (sampling/parameter sensitivity), integration with constraint propagation/zero compression, and more extensive evaluation on large real-world domains.
